---
title: "One-stage and two-stage models for binary outcomes"
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{one-stage-two-stage-binary-jackson}
  %\VignetteEngine{quarto::html}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

## Introduction

Binary outcome meta-analyses can be performed using either
**two-stage** or **one-stage** approaches.

The two-stage method first computes study-level effect sizes,
then performs meta-analysis.

The one-stage method models the original likelihood directly.

## Two-stage model

First compute the log odds ratio:

\[
y_i = \log(OR_i)
\]

Then perform random-effects meta-analysis:

\[
y_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i, s_i^2)
\]

\[
\theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2)
\]

## One-stage model (Jackson et al.)

For study \(i\) and arm \(j\):

\[
e_{ij} \sim \text{Binomial}(n_{ij}, \pi_{ij})
\]

with logit link

\[
\text{logit}(\pi_{ij}) = \gamma_i + j\theta + z_{ij}\epsilon_i
\]

where

- \(\gamma_i\) = baseline risk
- \(\theta\) = treatment effect
- \(\epsilon_i\) = heterogeneity term

### Assumptions

- binomial likelihood
- logit link
- study-specific baseline risks
